prompt:
A historian says the claim is “circumscribed,” since it applies confidently only to towns whose customs logs survive in full.
As used in the text, what does “circumscribed” most nearly mean?

choices.A: limited in scope
choices.B: celebrated by scholars
choices.C: difficult to summarize
choices.D: morally questionable

explanation: The claim applies only to certain places with surviving records, so it is limited in scope.

prompt:
A scholar argues that captions in excavation reports do more than identify photographs. By labeling one trench “disturbed fill” and another “sealed surface,” editors can suggest which evidence readers should treat as especially reliable before readers have fully examined the diagrams themselves. In that way, captions guide interpretation as well as reference.
Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.A: Captions in excavation reports can influence how readers judge evidence, not just identify images.
choices.B: Excavation reports are useful only when every trench photograph has the same caption.
choices.C: Readers usually ignore captions once they begin studying diagrams closely.
choices.D: Editors should avoid labeling trench photographs altogether.

explanation: The passage says the labels encourage readers to treat some evidence as more reliable before evaluating it themselves.

prompt:
Music historians sometimes use ticket ledgers to estimate attendance at benefit concerts. Yet complimentary admissions for donors, reporters, and performers’ guests often produced no ticket purchase record. As a result, the ledgers may understate the actual number of people who attended.
Which choice can most reasonably be inferred from the text?

choices.A: Ticket ledgers may provide an incomplete estimate of attendance at benefit concerts.
choices.B: Complimentary admissions were usually more numerous than ticket sales at benefit concerts.
choices.C: Reporters were always admitted for free to every public performance.
choices.D: Historians can calculate the exact number of complimentary admissions from ticket ledgers alone.

explanation: Complimentary admissions were not recorded as purchases, so the ledgers may undercount attendance.

prompt:
A critic notes that an essay repeatedly describes footnotes as hinges, brackets, and joints. The critic argues that this language presents scholarly writing as something that connects parts of an argument while also allowing movement between them.
Which choice best describes the function of the first sentence in the text?

choices.A: It introduces a repeated metaphor that the second sentence interprets.
choices.B: It summarizes the essay’s argument in chronological order.
choices.C: It rejects the interpretation proposed in the second sentence.
choices.D: It explains how hardware is manufactured for doors and cabinets.

explanation: The first sentence names the metaphor, and the second explains its significance.

prompt:
A student is writing about ventilated facades and has taken these notes:
• Some building exteriors use an air gap behind the outer cladding.
• The gap can help reduce heat buildup and moisture accumulation.
• However, performance depends on climate and installation quality.
• Vents and drainage paths must be kept clear through regular maintenance.
The student wants to emphasize both a potential benefit of ventilated facades and a factor that can limit their effectiveness. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.A: Ventilated facades can reduce heat buildup and moisture accumulation, but they depend on climate, installation quality, and maintenance.
choices.B: Ventilated facades eliminate the need for any other building maintenance.
choices.C: Climate has no effect on how well ventilated facades work.
choices.D: An air gap behind cladding always keeps buildings completely dry.

explanation: This choice accurately combines the stated benefits and limitations.

prompt:
The editor expected the revised train schedule to reduce missed connections. ______, many commuters continued relying on the older platform numbers they had memorized.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

choices.A: For example,
choices.B: Instead,
choices.C: Similarly,
choices.D: Therefore,

explanation: The actual outcome contrasts with the editor’s expectation.

prompt:
The diary cannot be from 1904<u>: it references a subway station that opened in 1911.</u>
Which choice best punctuates the underlined portion of the text?

choices.A: NO CHANGE
choices.B: ; it references a subway station that opened in 1911
choices.C: , it references a subway station that opened in 1911
choices.D: —it references a subway station that opened in 1911

explanation: The colon correctly introduces the specific fact that proves the date impossible.

prompt:
Far from serving merely as a boundary, the low stone wall also guided movement through the courtyard, framed views of the harbor, and marked who belonged inside the space.
Which choice best describes the function of the introductory phrase in the text?

choices.A: It contrasts a limited description of the wall with a broader explanation of its role.
choices.B: It identifies the mason who built the wall.
choices.C: It explains how the wall was repaired after a storm.
choices.D: It presents the results of a survey of courtyard visitors.

explanation: The introductory phrase rejects a narrow view of the wall as merely a boundary.

prompt:
After Mina spoke with Rafael about Chloe’s outline, <u>she</u> suggested moving the case study to the opening section.
Which choice best revises the text to avoid ambiguity?

choices.A: she
choices.B: Mina
choices.C: they
choices.D: someone

explanation: Replacing the pronoun with “Mina” removes the ambiguity.

prompt:
Had the census distinguished resident apprentices from seasonal laborers, the workshop totals <u>would be</u> easier to interpret today.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: would be
choices.B: would have been
choices.C: are
choices.D: were

explanation: The sentence describes an unreal past condition with a present result.

prompt:
The editor intended to say that nearly every page of the notebook was reproduced.
Which choice most effectively conveys the intended meaning?

choices.A: The archivist nearly reproduced every page of the notebook.
choices.B: The archivist reproduced nearly every page of the notebook.
choices.C: The archivist reproduced every nearly page of the notebook.
choices.D: The archivist reproduced every page nearly of the notebook.

explanation: “Nearly” should modify “every page,” not the act of reproduction.

prompt:
[1] Managers sometimes install rows of brush bundles across bare dunes. [2] The bundles slow wind and trap blowing sand. [3] As sand accumulates, grasses can root more easily. [4] Over time, the dune surface may rise enough to better protect inland areas from storms.
Which choice best describes the most logical placement for the following sentence?
“The bundles are meant to stabilize the dune while also creating conditions that support plant growth.”

choices.A: Before sentence 1
choices.B: After sentence 2
choices.C: After sentence 3
choices.D: After sentence 4

explanation: The added sentence best follows the explanation of erosion control and leads into the benefit for fish habitat.

prompt:
A researcher compared the average number of minutes four groups spent discussing a planning proposal.
Group A: 25
Group B: 19
Group C: 22
Group D: 17
A student claims that Group D spent the least time discussing the proposal, on average. Which choice best evaluates the student’s claim?

choices.A: The claim is unsupported because Group B also spent less than twenty minutes.
choices.B: The claim is supported because Group D’s average is lower than those of the other groups.
choices.C: The claim is unsupported because Group C spent more time than Group A.
choices.D: The claim is supported because Group D spent less time than Group A but more than Group C.

explanation: Group D’s 17 minutes is the lowest value in the table.

prompt:
The reviewer calls the model “parsimonious,” noting that it explains the data without introducing unnecessary assumptions.
As used in the text, what does “parsimonious” most nearly mean?

choices.A: economical in its assumptions
choices.B: highly emotional
choices.C: difficult to test
choices.D: visually appealing

explanation: “Parsimonious” means using no more assumptions than necessary.

prompt:
In a novel, the narrator says, “After the observatory closed, my aunt still dusted the brass speaking tube each Thursday. No instructions would travel through it now, but it kept the stairwell listening for a voice.”
Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.A: The routine preserves a sense of communication and purpose after the observatory has closed.
choices.B: The narrator plans to reopen the observatory and use the speaking tube again.
choices.C: The speaking tube is kept only because it is made of brass.
choices.D: The stairwell matters chiefly because it improves sound inside the building.

explanation: The tube no longer carries messages, but the ritual keeps alive the sense that it still could.

prompt:
A study found that geology students who grouped fossil diagrams by mode of preservation later remembered which fossils were structurally related more accurately than students who reviewed the same diagrams alphabetically by fossil name. The researchers note, however, that the study did not test whether the grouping activity improved memory for excavation sites.
Which choice can most reasonably be inferred from the text?

choices.A: Grouping fossil diagrams by mode of preservation may help students remember relationships even if its effect on excavation-site memory is uncertain.
choices.B: Alphabetical order is never useful for studying geology.
choices.C: Excavation sites are always easier to remember than fossil relationships.
choices.D: The study proved that grouping is the best strategy for every science lesson.

explanation: The study found improved memory for structural relationships but did not test excavation-site recall.

prompt:
A literary historian argues that harbor timetables influenced some modernist prose. To support this claim, the historian points to a passage that presents departures largely through times, destinations, and cargo types, making the prose resemble a printed schedule more than a conventional narrative scene.
Why does the author most likely include the example of the passage presenting departures through times, destinations, and cargo types?

choices.A: To illustrate what timetable-like prose looks like in literature
choices.B: To prove that harbor timetables were more widely read than modernist fiction
choices.C: To argue that schedules are always more realistic than narrative scenes
choices.D: To suggest that all modernist prose copied transportation documents directly

explanation: The example shows how a passage can resemble a harbor schedule.

prompt:
A student is writing about after-hours refrigerated share lockers at a campus farm and has taken these notes:
• Some campus farms install refrigerated lockers for produce-share pickups.
• The lockers let members retrieve produce outside staffed hours.
• However, the lockers require electricity, cleaning, and temperature monitoring.
• Their usefulness also depends on where they are placed and how many compartments they have.
The student wants to explain a potential benefit of the lockers while acknowledging a practical limitation. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.A: Refrigerated share lockers can make produce pickup more flexible, but they require power, upkeep, and careful placement.
choices.B: Refrigerated lockers make staffed pickup hours unnecessary in every case.
choices.C: Locker placement has no effect on how useful the system is.
choices.D: Temperature monitoring is unimportant once produce has been placed in a locker.

explanation: This choice combines the benefit of flexible pickup with the limitations described in the notes.

prompt:
The panel agreed that the introductory label needed revision. ______, members disagreed about whether the new version should preserve technical vocabulary or explain more terms for general readers.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?

choices.A: Consequently,
choices.B: Nevertheless,
choices.C: Similarly,
choices.D: For example,

explanation: The second sentence contrasts agreement on the need for revision with disagreement about how to revise.

prompt:
The seminar asks students to compare sources, question assumptions, and <u>map how each revision alters the claim.</u>
Which choice best explains why the underlined portion is effective?

choices.A: It correctly completes a parallel series with the verbs “compare” and “question”.
choices.B: It should be changed to “mapping how each revision alters the claim” to sound more formal.
choices.C: It is incorrect because every verb in a series must begin with “to”.
choices.D: It should be changed to “the mapping of how each revision alters the claim” to be grammatical.

explanation: “Map” matches the base-form verbs in the series.

prompt:
The register <u>that omits the winter shipments</u> is probably incomplete.
Which choice best describes the underlined portion of the sentence?

choices.A: It is a nonessential phrase that should be set off with commas.
choices.B: It is a restrictive clause that correctly identifies which register is being discussed.
choices.C: It creates a comma splice.
choices.D: It should be moved to the end of the sentence for clarity.

explanation: The clause specifies a particular register rather than adding extra information.

prompt:
The sequence of closures and rushed repairs <u>has</u> weakened confidence in the bridge inspection schedule.
Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

choices.A: have
choices.B: has
choices.C: having
choices.D: are

explanation: The singular subject “sequence” takes the singular verb “has.”

prompt:
A student wants to revise a sentence for precision:
“The new archive request form improved the reading room.”
Which choice best improves the sentence by making it more precise?

choices.A: The new archive request form improved the reading room.
choices.B: The new archive request form changed the reading room in several ways.
choices.C: The new archive request form grouped similar requests, reduced staff clarification time, and made incomplete submissions easier to spot.
choices.D: The new archive request form made the reading room much better overall.

explanation: This choice replaces a vague claim with specific improvements.

prompt:
[1] On sea cliffs, narrow ledges may collect windblown soil while adjacent rock remains bare. [2] Mosses often establish first on those ledges. [3] As they thicken, they hold more moisture and fine material, making the ledges more favorable for later plants. [4] Ecologists therefore map such cliffs at very fine scales rather than treating them as uniform rock faces.
Which choice best describes the most logical placement for the following sentence?
“Even slight differences in shelter can therefore shape where plant communities begin.”

choices.A: Before sentence 1
choices.B: After sentence 2
choices.C: After sentence 3
choices.D: After sentence 4

explanation: The added sentence best follows the explanation of how ledges become more favorable for plants and leads into the broader ecological conclusion.

prompt:
The report originally described the orientation help desk as “busy,” but that wording failed to show that it was busiest among first-year students during the first week of classes, while late-semester traffic was much lighter. The editor suggests replacing “busy” with <u>busiest among first-year students during the first week of classes</u>.
Which choice best describes the effect of the revision?

choices.A: It makes the statement more precise by specifying who used the help desk most and when.
choices.B: It makes the statement less formal by using conversational language.
choices.C: It changes the meaning by suggesting that the help desk closed later in the semester.
choices.D: It introduces a personal opinion that the original did not contain.

explanation: The revision identifies both the main users and the busiest period.

prompt:
In an experiment, participants reconstructing a repeating mosaic border made fewer placement errors when the reference image was divided into labeled squares than when the same border appeared without square labels. The researchers suggest that the labeled squares helped participants keep track of position.
Which choice is best supported by the text?

choices.A: Labeled squares may help people reconstruct a repeating mosaic more accurately.
choices.B: Mosaic borders cannot be copied correctly without written instructions.
choices.C: The labeled version was easier only because it used fewer colors.
choices.D: The experiment proved that labels improve every kind of visual task.

explanation: The passage directly reports fewer errors when the reference image used labeled squares.

prompt:
An essay on marked blueprints ends by noting that erased measurements, penciled substitutions, and shifted doorways reveal how designers revised a building plan over time. For that reason, some architectural archives preserve working blueprints alongside the final printed plans.
Which choice best describes the function of the final sentence in the text?

choices.A: It applies the earlier idea to explain a preservation practice.
choices.B: It narrows the discussion to the chemistry of blueprint paper.
choices.C: It argues that working blueprints are always more valuable than printed plans.
choices.D: It repeats the earlier claim without extending it.

explanation: The final sentence explains why architectural archives preserve working blueprints in light of what those revisions reveal.
